coredata.dc:title: Copper nanoclusters combined with polymer films as highly sensitive colorimetric probes for visual and proportional fluorescence detection of hexavalent chromium ions in pH and natural waters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

Hou, Haoting
Zhang, Zhe

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel type of fluorescent nanocomposite film was prepared, which could be used for pH value sensing and detecting heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions. Copper nanoclusters doped into polyvinyl alcohol polyethylene glycol fluorescent nanocomposite film (CuNCs/PVA/PEG) has excellent fluorescent properties; it can not only be used as a pH sensor but also can selectively detect heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions. When pH>9, the CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film will be quenched, which can be used as pH sensing. In addition, the CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film can detect heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions with a detection line of 0.01 μM. It shows that the probe can detect Cr6+ quickly, sensitively, and selectively and has broad application prospects in environmental analysis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer film
Fluorescence
Copper nanocluster
Cr6+ detection

Procedures for fluorescence measurements of Cr6+ ions
Synthesis and characterization of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film
Optical properties and fluorescence stability of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film
Fluorescence response of pH to CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of CuNCs, scale bar: 20 nm. (b) The size distribution shows that the average size is 3.8±0.1 nm.
(a) SEM image of the PVA/PEG/CuNCs film. (b) XRD of PVA/PEG and CuNCs/PVA/PEG film. (c) XPS spectra of CuNCs. (d) XPS spectra of copper in CuNCs.

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film. (Inset: the photographs of fluorescence under room light (left) and under 365 nm UV light illumination (right). (b) Normalized PL emission spectra of DPA-CuNCs at excitation wavelengths progressively increasing from 330 to 400 nm. (c) Ultraviolet absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CuNCs (λex=350 nm, λem=649 nm); (d) UV-Vis absorption of PVA/PEG and CuNCs/PVA/PEG film.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film in different pH conditions (1−14). (b) Line graph of fluorescence intensity of CuNCs/PVA/PEG under different pH (1−14). (c) Photographs of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film in different pH conditions (1−14) in sunlight (top) and under 365 nm UV light illumination (bottom).

The relative fluorescence intensity of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film in the presence of 50 μM different metal ions (Hg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Cr6+, Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Ni+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Al3+), F: the fluorescence intensity of the Cr6+-containing nanocomposite at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, F0: the fluorescence intensity of without Cr6+ nanocomposite at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film different concentrations of Cr6+ (from 0 μM to 100 μM, λex=350 nm, λem=649 nm). (b) Line graph of fluorescence intensity of CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film in the presence of addition of different concentration of Cr6+ ranging from 0 to 100 μM. (c) Photographs of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film in the presence of different concentrations of Cr6+ in sunlight (top) and under 365 nm UV light illumination (bottom).
The preparation process of CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film, the pH fluorescence sensing and the detection of Cr (VI).

head.ce:title: Copper nanoclusters combined with polymer films as highly sensitive colorimetric probes for visual and proportional fluorescence detection of hexavalent chromium ions in pH and natural waters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun 130117, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun, 130117, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of PICU, Children's Medical Center, The First Hospital of Jilin University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of PICU, Children's Medical Center, The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of PICU, Children's Medical Center, The First Hospital of Jilin University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel type of fluorescent nanocomposite film was prepared, which could be used for pH value sensing and detecting heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions. Copper nanoclusters doped into polyvinyl alcohol polyethylene glycol fluorescent nanocomposite film (CuNCs/PVA/PEG) has excellent fluorescent properties; it can not only be used as a pH sensor but also can selectively detect heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions. When pH>9, the CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film will be quenched, which can be used as pH sensing. In addition, the CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film can detect heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions with a detection line of 0.01 μM. It shows that the probe can detect Cr6+ quickly, sensitively, and selectively and has broad application prospects in environmental analysis applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: We have produced a novel orange-red fluorescent emitting CuNCs and the resulting CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent composite film is hydrophilic, highly stable and exhibits long-wavelength emission in the red region. Importantly, the fluorescent composite film allows specific detection of hexavalent chromium in real water samples with a detection limit of 0.01 μM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is commonly used in the industrial production of alloys and tanning agents and has significant commercial value. However, the public is more aware of the harmful effects of Cr on the environment than its potential uses [1]. Different valences of Cr have additional toxicity. Cr has three forms in the background, namely Cr(0), Cr(III), and Cr(VI), but compared with Cr(0) and Cr(III), Cr(VI) is highly toxic to animals and humans [2]. With the development of the chemical industry, domestic and industrial wastewater is discharged irresponsibly into rivers, causing serious harm to water resources. Cr(VI) is a persistent pollutant in various water sources. It poses serious health risks and has been regulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), which sets the Maximum Contaminant Level Target (MCLG) for total chromium concentration in drinking water at 0.589 μM [3]. Cr(III) is non-toxic and is an essential trace element for the human body to maintain the average glucose tolerance factor and protein, lipid, and fat metabolism. Therefore, high-sensitivity detection of Cr(VI) is of great significance, which helps distinguish harmful Cr(VI) from benign Cr(III). Thus, rapid and sensitive Cr(VI) detection urgently needs simple, reliable, and efficient detection technology. At present, there are many methods for detecting Cr(VI), such as electrochemical technology [4]. atomic absorption spectrometry [5], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [6], colorimetry [7] and fluorescence methods, etc. [8]. However, these methods have limitations, such as narrow detection range, cumbersome operation, and expensive equipment. Though many new methods for detecting Cr(VI) using metal nanoclusters as fluorescent probes have recently been described [9–11], there are many deficiencies in these detection methods. Developing a new detection method with good detection selectivity, low detection limit, and simple operation is necessary.

Since the pH value plays a vital role in biomedicine, detecting pH values has attracted more and more attention. For example, pH is essential for synthesizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) in biological systems, and a proton gradient drives ATP [12]. Therefore, it is precious to establish a new simple, and effective pH fluorescence sensor. So far, it has been reported that using their unique structure and photophysical characteristics, a variety of nanoscale materials can be used for pH sensings, such as carbon dots(CDs) [13], quantum dots (QDs) [3,14,15], modified nanoparticles [16], and metal nanoclusters.

Metal Nano Clusters have caused widespread concern about their excellent optical stability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [17–20]. In recent years, different stabilizers have synthesized a variety of Au and Ag nanocrystals, and the research on non-precious metal Cu nanocrystals is limited [21]. Compared with Au and Ag, Cu is very cheap, ordinary, and generally concerned [22,23]. However, it quickly oxidizes in the air, causing instability [24,25]. So far, copper clusters (CuNCs) synthesized with different stabilizers have been used for other purposes. Qian et al. prepared blue fluorescent protective copper nanocrystals for a one-step method in an aqueous solution for reversible pH sensors [26]. Hongding Zhang and Wang Haibo et al. synthesized a type of copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) stabilized with highly luminescent glutathione using GSH as a stabilizing agent and ascorbic acid (AA) as a reducing agent [27,28]. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) were prepared using adenine thymine (AT)-rich single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) as a template by Ningning Wu et al. [29]. Bhamore et al. developed a green microwave-assisted method for synthesizing copper nanoclusters having orange emission and serving as a fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of Thiram and Paraquad in water and food samples [30].

Water-soluble polymers of polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) are very important because these polymers have biocompatibility and biodegradability and also have many applications, such as cosmetics and packaging, etc [31,32]. Some modifications are made to existing polymers to improve their optical, structural, and morphological properties. Various methods such as block copolymers, polymer blends, plasticizers, and inert fillers are used to enhance the performance of the polymer matrix. Photoluminescent film has a wide range of applications in solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and sensors [33–35]. In particular, polymer fluorescence sensing film has good stability, convenient operation, regeneration, and so on [36]. Up to now, various fluorescent polymer films have been reported and applied in the sensing field. Pradeep M et al. studied the AuNCs nanocomposite membrane to detect Cu2+ in an aqueous solution [37–39]. Zhang et al. developed a single-stranded DNA photonic crystal (SSDNA-PC) fluorescent membrane for the detection of Hg2+ [37]. Lin and colleagues designed a dual emission ratio fluorescence sensing membrane for metal ions detection. The film was prepared from chitosan, graphitized carbon nitride (G-C3N4), and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [40]. There are still some problems in the fluorescent polymer sensing films prepared in the above studies, including the miscibility of fluorescent nanomaterials, the quenching caused by the polymerization, and the low transparency of the films.

Here, the preparation process of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film device is shown in Scheme 1. Copper nanoclusters were synthesized by the complex redox method using penicillamine (DPA) as a template. The prepared copper nitrate solution was added drop by drop into the penicillamine solution and stirred in a magnetic stirrer for 2 hours. During stirring, when the color of the substrate changed from brown to milky white, different volumes of aqueous copper cluster solution were added to the same volume of PVA/PEG solution using a pipette. Stirring was continued for half an hour with a magnetic stirrer to incorporate the copper nanoclusters into the PVA/PEG polymer matrix. Finally, the mixture was evenly poured into the polytetrafluoroethylene mold at room temperature and placed for 24 h to form a film. The CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film can be obtained by gently peeling it off slowly with tweezers. In recent years, though the number of studies on optically active gold and silver nanostructures has increased significantly, the understanding of optically active copper nanoclusters and their potential applications is still limited. The fluorescent nanocomposite film prepared by doping penicillamine stabilized copper nanoclusters into PVA/PEG composite film has not been reported. The CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film has excellent fluorescence characteristics; it is selective for heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions but it can also be used as a sensor to detect the pH of aqueous solutions. In the environmental field, seeing heavy metal ions is of great significance.

Distilled water was used during the experiment. D-penicillamine (DPA) and analytical grade copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2, 99.5 %) were obtained from Changchun San-bang Pharmaceutical Technology Co, Ltd. PBS, glycerin, Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, Mwt = 31,000 g/mol, Aldrich), polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mwt = 4000 g/mol, Shanghai Reagent No. 1 Factory).

In the case of CuNCs/PVA/PEG film, the above similar mixture was then stirred on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature for half an hour after the addition of copper nanoparticles in different ratios concerning the PVA/PEG mixture according to the following formula:
x
wt %
=
w
f
w
f
+
w
p
Wf and Wp are the weights of copper nanoparticles and PVA/PEG blend, respectively. Pour the solution into a petri dish and leave it at room temperature for 1 day until the polymer film is formed.

A pair of orange luminescent CuNCs with photoactivity was successfully prepared by the complexation and redox reaction of Cu(II) and d-penicillamine (DPA) enantiomers, in which DPA is both a reducing agent and a stable ligand. At room temperature, 100 μL of 150 mM Cu (NO3)2 and 3 mL of 25 mM DPA were added to 1.9 mL of deionized water. After the two reactants were mixed, the solution quickly turned brown. After 2 hours of reaction, the 9500 rpm was centrifuged for 15 min, and the milky residue was separated and washed repeatedly 3 times.

1 mL of phosphate buffer saline (pH = 7.4), 2 mL of 5 % polyethylene glycol (PEG) solution, and 2 mL of glycerin were added in turn. The solution was heated at 90 °C until it completely dissolved, and reflux was heated at 90 °C for 30 min. Finally, each proportion of the mixed film was stirred under a magnetic stirrer (700 r/min) for 30 minutes and then spread out on a petri dish, placed in a drying oven (30 °C) for 5 hours, taken it out, and put it on the PTFE film-coated board, place it at room temperature for 10 h to form a film (Synthesis mechanism see Fig. S1). Through research, it was found that the doping concentration of nanoparticles directly affects the optical parameters of the composite film. CuNCs with a mass fraction of 1.3 % are doped into PVA/PEG to form the film with the best performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescence measurements of pH value sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, solutions of different pH (1–14) were prepared by diluting HCl and NaOH solutions at a concentration of 1 mol/L. The CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite membranes were immersed in these pH solutions, absorbed for 2 minutes, and then removed, and finally fluorescence detection was carried out. A series of fluorescence spectra were recorded under 350 nm excitation with the excitation and emission slits of the fluorescence spectrometer set at 5.0 and 5.0 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescence measurements of Cr6+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: A good linear relationship was obtained in the low concentration range of Cr6+ with a detection limit of 1 × 10−5 M in an aqueous medium. This result suggests the potential application of CuNCs /PVA/PEG as a probe in monitoring Cr6+ in environmental security. A simple method for preparing high emissivity DPA-CuNCs was used to detect Cr6+ ions. Put a Cr6+ (0.01 M) standard aqueous solution was designed and diluted to the required concentration of 1 × 10−3 μM∼100 μM. Then mix a series of Cr6+ ions solutions of different engagements with the working solution at room temperature for a few minutes. The Cr6+ calibration chart was obtained by plotting the relationship between F/F0 and Cr6+ concentration, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of nanocomposites containing Cr6+ and without Cr6+ at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, respectively. The series of fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 350 nm, and the excitation slit and emission slit of the fluorescence spectrometer were set at 5.0/5.0 nm, respectively.

A Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA) was used to study the microstructure and size distribution of CuNCs. A SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to study the internal structure of CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV-Vis-NIR) spectrometer (Agilent, USA). A Nicolet IS50 Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film surface functional groups. An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for photoluminescence spectrum (PL) analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) shows that CuNCs particles are spherical and have good dispersion and uniform particle size distribution. The average particle size is about 3.8 nm in Fig. 1(b). The EDS spectrum (Fig. S2) shows that the main components of the sample are carbon (64.7 %), oxygen (21.6 %), and copper (1.7 %). The collected images displayed the elemental mapping in different colors for Cu (red) and C (green). The basic mapping images confirmed the uniform distribution of CuNCs in the PVA/PEG film [41–43]. The PVA/PEG films alone are well-blended, flexible, and hydrophilic. As shown in Fig. S3, the doping of aqueous-phase copper clusters in PVA/PEG hybrid polymers promotes the cross-linking of the composite films by interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, during the film-forming process. The CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent composite films were found to have a more homogeneous and wetter surface microstructure than the PVA/PEG film films, with a smoother surface, higher transparency, and almost unaffected hydrophilicity, as shown in Fig. S4 [44].

From Fig. 2(a), it can be seen that the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film is well blended, with a smooth surface and good flexibility. Fig. 2(b) shows that the doping of copper nanoclusters into PVA/PEG thin film hasn't changed the original crystal structure of the PVA/PEG thin film, and the doped film has a diffraction peak around 2θ=41.08°, that is no obvious combination effect between the copper clusters and the film substrate[14,45,46]. In addition, the diffraction peak intensity and peak shape of XRD thin film have little change before and after doping, indicating that the process of forming CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film has no effect on the internal crystal structure of PVA/PEG film, which is mainly combined by intermolecular hydrogen bonding. To investigate the composition of CuNCs nano-composites, XPS test was characterized. It could be found that the samples contained C, N, O and Cu in XPS spectra (Fig. 2(c)). There are two distinctive distinct peaks at 934.6 eV and 955.2 eV in the XPS energy spectrum of 2p of Cu Fig. 2(d). The strong peak is 2p3/2 of Cu, and the gentle rise is 2p1/2; the 2p3/2 peak position represents zero-valent Cu, and the 2p1/2 peak position represents monovalent Cu. It can be concluded from the XPS plots that the valence of Cu in CuNCs is zero-valent Cu and monovalent Cu. At the same time, The absence of the characteristic peak for Cu(II) around 942.0 eV indicates the lack of Cu(II) in CuNCs, further demonstrating the successful synthesis of CuNCs [47,48]. Fig. S5 reveals that the FTIR spectra for all samples revealed a broad at 2936 cm−1 attributed to the stretching CH2 group. The infrared spectral bands observed at 1076 cm−1 and 847 cm−1 are attributable to the C-O-C ether bond and the stretching vibration of the C-C group [49–51]. The S-H stretching mode disappeared at 2511 cm−1 and the characteristic peaks of COO- stretching mode appeared at 1341 cm−1 and 1529 cm−1. In the vicinity of 1635 cm−1, a new absorption band appears in the infrared spectrum of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film, which corresponds to the N-H bending, suggesting that penicillamine is fixed on the cluster surface through Cu-S bonds. The S-H stretching mode disappeared at 2511 cm−1, and the characteristic peaks of COO- stretching mode appeared at 1341 cm−1 and 1529 cm−1 [52–55]. Finally, the band positions shifted to the existence of copper nanoparticles in the composite film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence stability of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG film
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent nanocomposite film prepared in this experiment has good fluorescence performance. Under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm wavelength, it emits orange fluorescence Fig. 3(a). As shown in the illustration, different excitation wavelengths have different emission intensities. Fig. 3(b) shows that the occurrence intensity is the highest when the excitation wavelength is 350 nm. The optimal excitation of the copper clusters is 350 nm, the maximum emission is 649 nm, and the copper nanoclusters have an absorption peak of around 300 nm [39,56]. The single PVA/PEG film has no absorption peak, while the fluorescent nanocomposite film with CuNCs has an absorption peak at about 300 nm, which confirms the formation of CuNCs/ PVA/PEG in Fig. 3(c). Fig. S6(a-c) shows that the fluorescence of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG composite film is very stable. After placing it for 8 weeks or irradiating it with a 365 nm UV lamp for 60 minutes, there is almost no change in the fluorescence intensity. The CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film has a certain salt tolerance. When the film is placed in a NaCl solution of different concentrations, the concentration range is from 0 to 30 mM, and its fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of pH to CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film decreased with the increase in pH value. When the pH>9, the fluorescence is quenched under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm, and the film color changes from orange to black. In sunlight, the color of the fluorescent nanocomposite film changes from milky white to black-brown. The reasons for this are as follows: the charge state of the penicillamine ligand immobilized on CuNCs can be determined by adjusting the pH of the medium, thereby affecting the formation of aggregates. In a high pH aqueous solution, the synthesis of CuNCs mediated by penicillamine will lead to deprotonation formation (COO-), which acts as an isolated species solution, resulting in a decrease in P.L. intensity. In the pH range of 9–14, the concentration of CuNCs aggregates decreases almost linearly due to the responsive decomposition of CuNCs aggregates[57].

Next, we will use the constructed fluorescent nanocomposite film for selective ion detection; the concentration of different metal ions is unified to 50 μm, including (Hg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Cr6+, Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+, K+, Fe3+, Ni+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Al3+). As shown in Fig. 5, it turns out that it has a specific response to Cr6+. Under the same condition, the influence of other metal ions is almost negligible. In addition, parallel experiments are carried out to study the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescent nanocomposite film. It is observed that the quenching caused by the mixing of Cr6+ and other metal ions is similar to that caused by blank Cr6+ in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. S7(a) and S7(b), the fluorescence intensity of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film did not undergo a strong change after the addition of other metal cations to the system, while the fluorescence of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film was quenched drastically by the addition of Cr6+, which indicates that the CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film has a selective specificity for Cr6+, on the basis of which an analytical method for sensitive detection of Cr6+ can be established. In order to explore the sensitivity of the CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film, the fluorescence intensity is measured after Cr6+ is added for a fixed time of 10 minutes. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the nanocomposite film is immersed in a Cr6+ solution, and it is found that as the Cr6+ concentration increases from 0 μM to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite film gradually decrease[58]. The fluorescence intensity of CuNCs/PVA/PEG nanocomposite film decreases with increased Cr6+ concentration in Fig. 6(b). The relative fluorescence intensity was linear in the presence of very small amounts of Cr6+ ions up to a concentration of Cr6+ ions of 10 μM, and the linear equation is y = 190.69–48.506x, where R2 = 0.9986，and the detection limit of Cr6+ concentration was 0.01 μM. The fitted linear curve equation is
(
F
0
− F ) /
F
0
= 190.69 - 48.596
Cr 6 +
. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent composite films in the absence and presence of Cr6+, respectively, and the limit of detection (LOD) is determined by the "3 s/k" equation. where s is the standard deviation of the blank sample and k is the slope of F0/F. The limit of detection (LOD) for hexavalent chromium ions was calculated to be 0.01 μM. Table S1 shows that the CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent nanocomposite film is superior in detecting hexavalent chromium. Fig. 6(c) shows that the color of the nanocomposite film changes from off-white to red-brown under sunlight and is wholly quenched under ultraviolet light.

Large amounts of hydroxyl groups are present in the chemical structure of PVA/PEG. Using penicillamine as a stabilizer, CuNCs are synthesized. When the PVA/PEG chemical reaction occurs, the PVA/PEG complex structure is formed, and the hydroxyl group is exposed to the outside. When the two adjacent hydroxyl ions of the PVA/PEG complex structure are ionized, they will be combined with the metal ions in the form of O anions to form a stable complex. Hydroxyl groups react with Cr6+ to form the complex [47,59,60]. Fluorescence quenching is caused by changing the structure of PVA/PEG composite film. A method for Cr6+ detection based on CuNCs/PVA/PEG is established.

In order to verify the application value of this CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent composite film in real water samples, the spiked recovery method was used in this work to demonstrate the effect of CuNCs/PVA/PEG fluorescent composite film on the detection of Cr6+ in real water samples. Laboratory tap water and lake water from Changchun South Lake were selected as real water samples for testing, and both of them were filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane without any other treatment before use. 0.05 μM, 0.05 μM, and 5 μM Cr6+ were added to the tap water and lake water, respectively. The results are shown in Table S2: although there was some interference, the actual results were satisfactory and the spiked recoveries were good.

In summary, the fluorescent nanocomposite film prepared in this study has good water solubility, high-intensity fluorescent emission, and stability. Under sunlight, when the pH value in the solution is more significant than 9, the color of the fluorescent nanocomposite film changes to black-brown, and the fluorescence is quenched under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation. This fluorescent nanocomposite film can also be used to detect heavy metal hexavalent chromium ions specifically. When the concentration of hexavalent chromium ions in the solution is more significant than 0.01 μM, and under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the fluorescent film will undergo fluorescence quenching. In the sun, the color of the film is red-brown. Therefore, the film has broad application prospects in environmental analysis applications.

Zhe Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Xudong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yidan Liang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Lei Wang: Visualization, Investigation. Haoting Hou: Supervision, Resources. Mao Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yao Wu: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52273122), the Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology (Grant No. 20220203060SF), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University (sklssm2024034).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135126.
